pathophysiology_summary: A hordeolum (stye) is an acute staphylococcal infection of an eyelash follicle/associated gland (external) or a meibomian gland (internal), producing a tender, focal, erythematous lid-margin nodule often with a pointing pustule; a foreign-body sensation is common. It is benign and usually points and drains spontaneously in 48–72h with warm compresses. The critical ground-truth task is to distinguish this focal lid-margin lesion from preseptal cellulitis (diffuse eyelid/periorbital erythema and swelling) and orbital cellulitis (proptosis, painful/limited eye movements, diplopia, reduced vision, systemic toxicity) — sight- and life-threatening emergencies — and to warn against squeezing the lesion (facial 'danger triangle' venous drainage risk).

differential.evidence_basis: Focal tender lid-margin lump with a pointing pustule and gritty sensation, background blepharitis.
Screen for diffuse (not focal) eyelid/periorbital erythema and swelling ± fever.
Screen for proptosis, painful/limited eye movements, diplopia, reduced vision — sight/life-threatening.
A chalazion is a painless chronic deeper nodule; the acute tenderness/pustule here favours hordeolum (may later evolve to a chalazion).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Hordeolum leads; preseptal/orbital cellulitis must be actively excluded before conservative reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Focal lid-margin lesion, no diffuse periorbital spread, painless eye movements, normal vision, no fever — uncomplicated stye.
No diffuse eyelid involvement; escalation target if spread develops.
Normal eye movements/vision, no proptosis; escalation target if features develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative orbital/preseptal screen, hordeolum leads; warm compresses and do-not-squeeze advice.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting with warm compresses; may evolve to a chalazion.

red_flags.description: Preseptal/orbital cellulitis — diffuse eyelid/periorbital erythema and swelling, proptosis, painful/limited eye movements, diplopia, reduced vision, fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orbital cellulitis is a sight- and life-threatening emergency needing IV antibiotics/imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Vision loss, cavernous sinus thrombosis, intracranial spread.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not distinguishing a focal lid lump from diffuse periorbital cellulitis with orbital signs.
Squeezing a facial 'danger-triangle' lesion risks spreading infection via valveless facial veins.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Slit-lamp/eversion examination of the lid and conjunctiva
Formal visual acuity, pupil and eye-movement assessment
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Warm compresses + lid hygiene, do not squeeze; urgent eye care/ED for periorbital spread or orbital signs.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Hordeolum: warm compresses and lid hygiene; most resolve spontaneously; topical antibiotics have limited role and oral antibiotics are reserved for associated cellulitis. Refer urgently for orbital signs.
